name: La Pola de Gordón Downhill Trail
name_original: Valle de Gordón – circular desde La Robla por la carretera de la mina de Santa Lucía
description: This difficult 21.6-mile touring cycling route from La Robla explores the Valle de Gordón, featuring mountain scenery and mining heritage.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around La Robla traverse a landscape characterized by diverse natural features, including dramatic gorges, river valleys, and mountain passes. The region's terrain offers a mix of paved and unpaved segments, catering to various cycling abilities. Cyclists can explore areas like the Valle de Gordón and enjoy views over the Bernesga Valley, with routes often following ancient paths and showcasing mining heritage. The area features impressive geological formations such as the Desfiladero de Los Calderones and Las Hoces de Vegacervera. Best touring cycling routes around La Robla The most popular touring cycling route is Guzmán del Bueno Fountain – Bridge of the Lions loop from La Robla, a 35.9 miles (57.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 46 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores historical elements and varied terrain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is La Robla Town Center – Ermita del Buen Suceso loop from La Robla, a moderate 15.5 miles (25.0 km) path. This route offers a range of difficulty levels and passes by the Ermita del Buen Suceso. Local touring cyclists also love the Ermita del Buen Suceso – La Muria mountain pass loop from Peredilla, a 12.0 miles (19.4 km) trail leading through mountain scenery, often completed in about 1 hour 50 minutes. Touring cycling around La Robla is defined by river valleys, mountain passes, and historical mining landscapes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging excursions. The routes in La Robla are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.8 stars from over 8 reviews. More than 275 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore La Robla's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around La Robla?
A: There are over 30 touring cycling routes documented around La Robla on komoot, offering a wide range of options for different skill levels and preferences.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in La Robla?
A: While most routes in La Robla are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy touring cycling route available. For those looking for a moderate option that can be adapted for varying difficulties, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14987035" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Robla Town Center – Ermita del Buen Suceso loop from La Robla&lt;/a&gt; offers a mix of terrain and is 25.0 km long.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, La Robla offers several difficult routes. One such option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1036911985" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Pola de Gordón Downhill Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 34.7 km and features significant elevation changes. Another demanding route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31549723" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alba Bridge – La Robla Town Center loop from La Robla&lt;/a&gt;, covering 31.6 km with considerable ascents.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling routes that pass through La Robla?
A: Yes, La Robla is part of the historic Vía de la Plata (Silver Route), a significant long-distance alternative pilgrimage route that traverses western Spain from south to north. This route has been enhanced for cycle tourism, offering an extensive journey through diverse landscapes.

Q: What natural attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes in La Robla?
A: La Robla's routes often feature stunning natural attractions. You can explore impressive geological formations like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4073598" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Calderones Gorge&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3541942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vegacervera Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall of the Arroyo de los Calderones&lt;/a&gt; is also a notable sight. Many routes offer views of the Valle de Gordón and the Bernesga Valley.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes available?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in La Robla are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24636220" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guzmán del Bueno Fountain – Bridge of the Lions loop from La Robla&lt;/a&gt;, a moderate 57.7 km ride, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38157711" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ermita del Buen Suceso – La Muria mountain pass loop from Peredilla&lt;/a&gt;, a more challenging 19.4 km option.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can be found along the cycling routes?
A: La Robla has a rich mining heritage, and some routes, like those through the Valle de Gordón, pass by historical sites such as the Santa Lucía mine road. You can also find unique religious sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558242" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hermitage of the Virgin of Manadero in the Cave of the Doves&lt;/a&gt;, a rupestrian sanctuary, and the Gruta de Nuestra Señora hermitage within Los Calderones Gorge.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in La Robla?
A: The touring cycling routes in La Robla are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.8 out of 5 stars. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from dramatic gorges to scenic river valleys, and the blend of natural beauty with historical mining heritage.

Q: What is the best season for touring cycling in La Robla?
A: The region around La Robla offers diverse terrain, making it enjoyable across different seasons. Spring and autumn generally provide pleasant temperatures and vibrant scenery. While some routes might be accessible in winter, especially on paved surfaces, higher mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6807331" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Muria mountain pass&lt;/a&gt; might be more challenging due to weather conditions.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of waterfalls?
A: Yes, you can find routes that lead to or pass by waterfalls. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5558248" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Waterfall of the Arroyo de los Calderones&lt;/a&gt; is a notable natural feature in the area, often accessible via routes exploring the Los Calderones Gorge.

Q: Can I find routes that explore the region's forests?
A: Absolutely. The area around La Robla features beautiful forested areas. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5235085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ciñera Beech Forest&lt;/a&gt; is a prominent natural highlight that can be incorporated into or viewed from various cycling routes, offering a serene and picturesque experience.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in La Robla?
A: The terrain in La Robla is quite varied. You can expect a mix of paved roads, suitable for traditional touring bikes, and unpaved segments that might require a more robust bike or good fitness. Routes often traverse river valleys, climb mountain passes, and navigate through dramatic gorges, offering a dynamic cycling experience.
tips: The landscape of La Robla has been marked for decades by its cement plant and its thermal power station. The first continues to operate at full capacity after a reform in 2004 that made it one of the most modern facilities of this type in Europe. The second was closed in 2020 as part of the closure of all coal-fired power plants in Spain, its two cooling towers being demolished in 2022 and radically changing the environment again, just as they did when they were built.
Site with water and tables for camping or resting on the road. It is located between the track and the highway.

Cultural and Religious Heritage: La Pola de Gordón has a number of historic churches and chapels, such as the Iglesia Nuestra Señora del Buen Suceso and the Ermita de Nuestra Señora de Celada, which are worth a visit for their architecture and history.

faqs.question: Where does the Valle de Gordón circular route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The circular route starts and ends in La Robla. You can typically find parking in the town center, such as near the La Robla Town Center, which serves as a convenient starting point for cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The route features mostly paved surfaces, which are generally good for touring bicycles. However, be prepared for some segments that may be unpaved and difficult, potentially requiring you to dismount and push your bike. This mix of surfaces adds to the challenging nature of the ride.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as a "Hard bike ride" and requires "very good fitness." While navigation is straightforward, the substantial elevation gain of nearly 570 meters and varied terrain make it challenging for beginners. Some sections might even require pushing your bike.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience stunning mountain scenery within the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve, a UNESCO-recognized area. Expect panoramic views of valleys and mountains, with opportunities to see trails, viewpoints, forests, and peaks. The route also passes through areas with a rich natural environment, ideal for discovering local fauna and flora.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest on the route?
faqs.answer: Key highlights along the route include the vibrant La Robla Town Center, the historic Ermita del Buen Suceso, and the La Pola de Gordón Town Center. The mention of the Santa Lucía mine road also hints at the region's mining heritage, adding a historical dimension to the natural beauty.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other well-known paths. Notably, it covers significant sections of the Camino del Salvador (33%) and the Vía de la Plata (19%). You'll also find it crosses parts of the Ruta de las Cuencas Mineras del Carbón (10%) and the Camino Olvidado (Puente Almuhey-La Robla) (8%).

faqs.question: Is the Valle de Gordón - circular from La Robla via the Santa Lucía mine road dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is primarily for touring bicycles, the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve is generally welcoming to dogs. However, given the challenging nature of the ride and potential for unpaved sections, it's best to ensure your dog is fit for such an adventure and always keep them on a leash, especially in natural reserve areas to protect wildlife.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is typically during the spring, early summer, or autumn months. These seasons offer more moderate temperatures and generally stable weather conditions, making the significant elevation gain and varied terrain more enjoyable. Avoid peak summer heat or winter snow and ice, which can make the route considerably more difficult.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle this route. It is freely accessible to the public. However, as it passes through the Alto Bernesga Biosphere Reserve, always adhere to local regulations regarding environmental protection and respect private property.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for completing this route is around 2 hours and 33 minutes. This is an estimate for experienced cyclists given the 'difficult' classification and significant elevation gain. Your actual time may vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and the specific conditions of the unpaved sections.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or restaurants near the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, both La Robla and La Pola de Gordón, which are towns along the route, offer amenities such as cafes and restaurants where you can refuel or relax. The La Robla Town Center and La Pola de Gordón Town Center are good places to look for these services.

waypoints.name: La Pola de Gordón Town Center
